facts: Rajasthan's ONLY Ashokan edict site — the Bhabru rock edict + Minor Rock Edict No. 1 were both issued from Bairat in the 3rd c. BCE.
The Bhabru edict is unique — it is the only Ashokan inscription that directly names Buddhist texts (7 works Ashoka commended to monks).
Ancient name 'Viratnagar' — the Mahabharata identifies this as King Virata's capital where the Pandavas spent their 13th year of exile in disguise.
The Bijak-ki-Pahari hill above the town has the ruins of India's earliest known circular Buddhist temple (chaitya), 3rd c. BCE.

properties.name: Kolvi Buddhist Caves
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + ASI Rajasthan Circle

facts: The largest group of rock-cut Buddhist caves in Rajasthan — ~50 caves cut into a laterite hill near Jhalarapatan.
Chaityas (prayer halls) and viharas (monastic cells) with a large seated Buddha in the main chaitya cave.
Contemporary with Ajanta's later phase (5-7th c.) — evidence that Buddhism survived in southeast Rajasthan long after it declined in the Ganges plain.
Adjoining caves at Vinayaka and Hathi-Gomph extend the same rock-cut monastic complex.

properties.name: Vinayaka / Ramgarh Buddhist Caves, Baran
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + ASI Rajasthan Circle

facts: Rock-cut Buddhist monastic complex near Ramgarh village — a sister site to Kolvi with the same 5-7th c. dating.
Ramgarh Crater — India's third recognised meteorite impact crater — sits ~10 km from the caves; the caves may have been carved into the crater's raised rim.
Together with Kolvi and the nearby Hathi-Gomph, these mark southeastern Rajasthan as Rajputana's principal Buddhist heartland.
